Preface.5
Plan of Catalog of Nuclear Reactor Concepts.6
List of Reactor Concepts.7
SECTION V.
REACTORS FUELED WITH URANIUM HEXAFLUORIDE, GASES, OR PLASMAS.9Chapter 1.
Introduction.9Chapter 2.
Reactors Fueled with Uranium Hexafluoride.13Chapter 3.
Reactors Fueled with Gases or Plasmas.37

Although uranium hexafluoride is normally a solid or a gas (it sublimes at $56.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under atmospheric pressure<sup>1</sup>) its use as a liquid by keeping the system under pressure has been proposed.
The liquid phase appears above the triple point of $147.3^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 22 psia.

The critical point is at $\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{c}}$ , 45.5 atm; and $\mathbf{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ , $446^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .<sup>2</sup> The reactors fueled with $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ are included in Chapter 2.
Reactors fueled with other gases or with plasmas are in Chapter 3; in some of these, uranium hexafluoride is the original form of the fuel.

Such problems as possible instability, corrosiveness, and fission-product deposition with $\mathrm{UF}_6$ and the need for special containment methods for gases and plasmas at extremely high temperatures will be covered in Chapters 2 and 3.
An early suggestion for the use of uranium hexafluoride as a reactor fuel was in the concept by Anderson and Brown in 1942.3 They proposed using liquid $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ .
In Russia, a low-power experimental reactor fueled with gaseous $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ began operation in 1957.4

text: The power given is 300 MW(t).
With D $_2$ O, 223 tubes, 8 cm ID, in cylindrical core 3.31 m long, 3.6 m diameter; 1.082 reproduction factor.
With graphite, 1700 tubes, 4 cm ID, 8.30 cm long.
Power: probably more than 100 MW(t).
Fuel circulates through 170 Al tubes surrounded by graphite to external heat exchanger.
Core requires 15 tons of UF<sub>6</sub> and 25 tons of graphite.
Fuel temperature in core: $194^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ ; temperature rise in center tube: $108^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ .
Tubes immersed in 25 tons of D $_2$ O surrounded by graphite reflector.
UF $_6$ enters at $176^{\circ}$ F and leaves at $248^{\circ}$ F.
Operating pressure: 250 psia.
Fuel-coolant-moderator: $U^{233}$ in UF $_6$ dissolved in fluorocarbon; circulates to external heat exchanger.
Temperature rise, fuel inlet to outlet: $90^{\circ}F$ .
Fuel-coolant: 686 tons liquid $\mathsf{UF}_6$ (465 tons U).
U, probably highly enriched in $U^{235}$ for burner, probably natural for breeder.
Moderator: 63 tons D $_2$ O.
Fertile material: $U^{238}$ in $\mathsf{UF}_6$ .
Maximum temperature: $150^{\circ}C$ , to avoid need for high pressure to keep $\mathsf{UF}_6$ liquid; minimum temperature: $75^{\circ}C$ , to prevent solidification.
Pressure: 150 psi, to prevent vaporization.
Specific power: 1300-1600 kW/ton $\mathsf{UF}_6$ at $\Delta T$ of $75 - 130^{\circ}C$ ; 550-650 kW/ton $\mathsf{UF}_6$ at $\Delta T$ of $75 - 105^{\circ}C$ .
Fuel-coolant-moderator: $U^{235}$ in liquid UF $_6$ dissolved in either liquid fluorocarbon or DF.
Reactor would operate at $100^{\circ}C$ and, with DF moderator, 15 atm.
Fuel-coolant: U $^{235}$ in liquid UF $_6$ ; could circulate to external heat exchanger.
Specific power: about 1 kW/kg UF $_6$ .

text: Reactor vessel: sphere, 17 m in diameter.
Fuel-coolant-moderator: enriched $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ with $\mathbf{F}_{2}$ added to stabilize molecular $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ and to act as additional moderator.

section: No. 17 Direct Expansion UF6-Fueled Reactor Metallgesellschaft Aktiengesellschaft
text: Pressure: 35 atm., high enough to begin chain reaction.
Fuel: $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ in gaseous $\mathbf{UF}_6$ .
Core structure: fuel in cylindrical core with 148 square Al channels, $40 \times 40$ cm, in square lattice under 1.3 atm.
Graphite reflector surrounding core is 50 cm thick on sides and 60 cm on top and bottom.
Criticality reached by heating fuel to $80 - 90^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , to cause a progressive increase in pressure.
Control: one horizontal and 4 vertical channels for control and safety rods--steel tubes filled with $\mathtt{B}_{4}\mathtt{C}$ .
Manual control: 2 tubular steel rods filled with $\mathtt{B}_{4}\mathtt{C}$ .
Power: about $1.5\mathrm{kW(t)}$ .
Fuel-coolant-fertile material: $8 \%$ enriched uranium in gaseous $\mathsf{U}\mathsf{F}_{6}$ with $\mathsf{BrF}_{3}$ added.
Core structure: core diameter 7.5 ft; height 25 ft.
Fuel, under 30 atm max. pressure, enters core at $450^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ and leaves at $900^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ .
Fuel flows by natural circulation; core height of 25 ft necessary for maintenance of sufficient fuel velocity.
Since fuel is $8 \%$ enriched, some Pu forms, but only as incidental product.

section: No. 21 Gaseous-Fuel Fast Breeder Reactor Worthington Corp.
text: Fuel: $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ in gaseous $\mathbf{UF}_6$ .

A concept for such reactors was described by Shepherd and Cleaver in 1949. $^{1}$ The advantage of a gaseous fuel, its capability for attaining temperatures too high for liquids or solids, is the basis for the suggested use of most of these concepts for rocket propulsion.
For interplanetary flights, the nuclear reaction would have to raise the propellant temperature by regenerative heating to about $10,000^{\circ}\mathrm{R}$ .
The name "cavity reactor" was suggested by Safonov $^{2}$ for those reactors in which a core of uniformly distributed gaseous fuel is surrounded by a moderator-reflector of uniform density.
Nelson<sup>3</sup> has suggested that, despite these problems, plasmas should be considered because of their advantages.
Limitations on the principal system variables in plasma systems are given in Table 1.<sup>3</sup>
In Shepherd and Cleaver's 1949 concept for rocket propulsion, the fuel and coolant are fed into a core area formed by the moderator-reflector.
The propellant-coolant must be heated to $3000 - 5000^{\circ}\mathrm{K}$ .
A specific power of $100\mathrm{MW}$ per metric ton was sought.
In 1955 Safonov $^{2}$ advanced a design for a cavity reactor, with different arrangements to fit different purposes.
The gaseous fuel, of low density at the high operating temperature of the reactor, could be $U^{233}$ or $U^{235}$ , as well as plutonium.
The gas- or air-cooled and the $UF_{6}$ -fueled reactors could be used to operate a turbine; the $UF_{6}$ -fueled reactor would operate by direct expansion.

The direct conversion of mechanical energy produced in a pulsing plasma to electrical energy is proposed in a 1957 concept by Colgate and Aamodt.4 The gaseous fuel, plutonium or uranium, is partially ionized at the temperature of operation.
The power is $500\mathrm{MW(e)}$ .
A high-temperature $(15,000^{\circ}\mathrm{K})$ reactor with electromagnetic confinement of fuel was described by Taylor in 1957.
The fuel, a plasma of $U^{233}$ , $U^{235}$ , or $P_u^{239}$ , is in a core surrounded by the moderator-reflector, heavy water or beryllium.
A preliminary design for a reactor of high power, called a "fizzler", was published by Bussard and DeLauer in 1958.6 Designed to produce 5000 MW(t), this reactor was intended for rocket propulsion.
Fuel (U $^{233}$ , U $^{235}$ , or Pu $^{239}$ ) and coolant (rocket propellant) mix in a core region formed by the unspecified moderator-reflector.
The authors conclude that at the operating pressure considered reasonable (1000 psi), greatly excessive amounts of fuel would be lost.
Two 1959 concepts by Grey were for rocket propulsion; few details were given.
In one, $^{8,9}$ a central moderator matrix contains the gaseous fuel and is surrounded by a solid fuel zone.
In the other, $^{10}$ the solid is in the central portion and the gaseous fuel in the moderator matrix surrounds it.
The concept for a vortex reactor, $^{11-17}$ proposed by Kerrebrock and Meghreblian, $^{11,12}$ emphasized the separation of fuel and propellant, after mixing, by the formation of a vortex.

Because of the radial pressure gradient within the vortex, the heavier fuel molecules would be contained in a steady-state concentration distribution in the vortex, while the lighter propellant gas would diffuse through it. $^{16}$ A core temperature of $10,000^{\circ}\mathrm{R}$ and a power of $500\mathrm{MW(t)}$ were sought.

Certain variables--tube diameter, mass flow rate per unit tube length, jet injection velocity, and wall pressure--affect vortex strength.[15] The strength increases as tube diameter decreases and as mass flow rate increases, and it increases with subsonic rather than supersonic injection.

They are: (1) bleed-off of the boundary layer through a uniformly porous wall, (2) injection of heavy gas uniformly through a porous wall, (3) wall cooling, and (4) bleeding off some of the total flow axially at radial positions between the tube center and the wall so that the exit radial flow is held to an allowable rate, based on diffusion considerations (bleed-off flow would be recirculated).[15]
One variant includes a rotating porous wall through which the propellant is introduced.[15] Turbulence is reduced because the boundary layer on the outer wall is effectively eliminated.

The third variant<sup>17</sup> is based on the same principle as the vortex reactor except that, to reduce turbulence and to dissipate instabilities, several vortices are generated in a single container in square array, by means of fluid injection from tubes located in the container.

In the Coaxial Flow Reactor of Weinstein and Ragsdale, $^{18,19}$ the fuel, fully ionized gaseous $\mathsf{Pu}^{239}$ , and the hydrogen coolant are fed into the core, which is surrounded by a moderator of graphite, heavy water, or both.
The average reactor temperature is about $10,000^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ .
The thermal energy must be attenuated by the propellant to avoid overheating the walls (maximum temperature $5000^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ ).
Because hydrogen is transparent to radiation between $5000^{\circ}$ and $12,000^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ , "seeding" with an absorber is necessary for radiative heat transfer.
Solid particles, such as graphite dust, might be adequate up to the sublimation point of graphite ( $6800^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ ).

A recent design by staff members at the Hanford Atomic Products Operation is for a system to produce electrical power by a magnetohydrodynamic generator and a secondary heat exchanger.[20] Both would derive their power from a reactor fueled with fissionable gas such as uranium hexafluoride.
A brief summary of a conference held at the Los Alamos Scientific Laboratory in April 1964 indicates recent work on some gas- and plasma-fueled reactors, although few details are given.[21]

section: No. 1 Gaseous Reactor for Rocket Propulsion
text: Fuel: material not specified, but could be in initial form of: (1) powder; (2) powder suspension in liquid; (3) wire extruded into chamber; or (4) liquid compound of fissile material.

section: No. 1 Gaseous Reactor for Rocket Propulsion
text: Coolant: $\mathsf{H}_2$ or $\mathsf{D}_2$ ; helium; ammonia, either $\mathsf{NH}_3$ or $\mathsf{N}^{15}\mathsf{D}_3$ ; or steam, either $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ or $\mathsf{D}_2\mathsf{O}$ .

section: No. 1 Gaseous Reactor for Rocket Propulsion
text: Reaction must heat coolant, which is rocket propellant, to 3000-5000 $^{\circ}$ K.

section: No. 1 Gaseous Reactor for Rocket Propulsion
text: Specific power: 100 MW/metric ton.
Fuel: $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ or $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ possibly in $\mathbf{UF}_6$ ; $\mathbf{Pu}^{239}$ .
Fuel density (presumably at operating conditions) between 0.05 and 0.10 lb/cu ft--less than $\frac{1}{4}$ molecular density of gases under standard conditions.
A 6-ft diameter core in Be or graphite would require 12-14 kg $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ for criticality; the same size in $\mathbf{D}_2\mathbf{O}$ would require 2 kg $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ .
Smaller amounts of $\mathbf{Pu}^{239}$ or $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ would be required.
Fuel might be $\mathbf{U}^{235}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ , which could expand directly for running a turbine or a reciprocating engine.
This conversion of mechanical energy to electrical is the basis for the generation of electrical power--500 MW(e).
Wall temperature is calculated to be 15,000°K or higher.

Containment: fuel confined within core region by externally applied electromagnetic fields, by either (1) simple magnetic bottle with mirrors at each end or (2) homopolar device providing a strong electrostatic field for which moderator-reflector is outer electrode and plasma is inner electrode.
Power density: 12-27 kW/cm $^3$ .
Fuel: $\mathrm{U}^{233}$ , $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ , or $\mathrm{Pu}^{239}$ initially in solid, liquid, or gaseous form but ultimately in gaseous form.
Fuel density needs to be about 0.10 lb/cu ft.
Power density: 100 MW/cu ft.
Drawback: at operating pressure of 1000 psi, which authors consider reasonable, 20,000 lb of fuel would be lost.
To lose only 300 lb, which is calculated to be a maximum amount, pressure of 67,000 psi would be needed; this is impractical.

Vortex can also be generated by: (1) externally imposed electrical and magnetic fields; (2) rotating magnetic field--a radial electric field between a pair of cylindrical electrodes combined with an axial magnetic field; or (3) magnetic mirror in a cylindrical configuration.
Gas exit pressure might be 100 atm., core temperature $10,000^{\circ}\mathrm{R}$ .
Fuel: fully ionized gaseous $\mathsf{Pu}^{239}$ .
Core structure: criticality takes place in cavity (10 ft long, 10 ft diameter.) formed by external moderator-reflector.
Fuel is fed into center of core; fuel region about 10 ft diam.

One study using both graphite and $\mathsf{D}_2\mathsf{O}$ as moderator-reflector has been based on the following parameters: reactor overall is 16.6 ft long and 16.6 ft in diameter, cavity: 10 ft long, 10 ft in diameter; moderator-reflector: 3.3 ft thick.
Fuel velocity: 0.2 ft/sec.
Coolant velocity: 17 ft/sec.
Temperatures: fuel, $20,000^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ ; hydrogen, $10,000^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ ; graphite, $5,000^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ ; $\mathsf{D}_2\mathsf{O}$ , $70^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ .
Graphite dust might be satisfactory up to its sublimation point ( $6800^{\circ}\mathsf{R}$ ).
Pressure: 2200 psia with fully ionized fuel.
